title: Arrhythmia — Ventricular Fibrillation
oneLiner: Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm; chaotic ventricular activity; immediate defibrillation is the only priority – survival drops ~10% per minute.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Asystole mimicking fine VF** – A flat line in one lead may be fine VF in another. **If any doubt, treat as VF and shock** (you cannot worsen asystole). Check lead II and aVF, increase gain.
- **Pseudo-VF from lead artifact / motion** – The patient will have a pulse or normal arterial line trace. **Never shock a patient with a pulse.** Feel for a carotid pulse before each rhythm check if the monitor looks like VF but patient is talking.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden collapse, unconsciousness, no response
  - Agonal respirations or absent breathing
  - Palpitations / sense of impending doom seconds before (rarely witnessed)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No palpable carotid pulse
  - Unresponsive – no eye opening, no verbal response, no motor response
  - Pupils dilated and fixed (after ~2 minutes)
  - Cyanosis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Ventricular Fibrillation (chaotic, irregular, no QRS)
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia** (regular wide QRS, usually monomorphic or polymorphic – same treatment: defibrillation)
- **Asystole** (flat line, may be confused with fine VF – use alternative lead, increase gain)
- **Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)** (organized rhythm but no pulse – treat underlying cause, do not shock)
- **Splitter:** **ECG rhythm strip** – coarse VF shows completely disorganised electrical activity; pulseless VT shows organised, regular wide-complex tachycardia. If any doubt, treat as shockable rhythm.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Immediate rhythm check via defibrillator pads – **do not delay defibrillation**
  - Check pulse manually before each defibrillator rhythm analysis
  - Confirm lead placement (RA, LA, RL, LL) if using a monitor – loose leads cause artifact
- **Bloods (post-ROSC / during arrest if IV access available):**
  - VBG/ABG (pH, lactate, pCO₂, K⁺)
  - U&Es (especially K⁺, Mg²⁺)
  - Troponin (if ischaemic cause suspected)
  - Full blood count, coagulation (after ROSC)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None during arrest – do not move patient for X-ray
  - **After ROSC:** CXR (check ET tube position, pneumothorax), CT head if neurological concern, coronary angiography if STEMI suspected
- **Special tests:** Echocardiography (done during pause in CPR if trained – look for tamponade, massive PE, LV function)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** VF is defined by ECG – no consensus scoring system; diagnosis is electrocardiographic
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG rhythm strip** – before charging, **confirm rhythm is truly VF (not artifact, not asystole)** and that the patient has no pulse. A single missed rhythm check can lead to shock in a perfusing rhythm or failure to shock a shockable rhythm.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Follow the ALS algorithm:**
    - Call for help / cardiac arrest team
    - Start CPR (30:2) immediately
    - Attach defibrillator pads (sternum-apex or anterior-posterior)
    - **Defibrillate as soon as VF/VT confirmed** – biphasic: 200 J (then 150–360 J per manufacturer)
    - After each shock, immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes before rhythm check
    - **After 3 shocks:** give IV adrenaline 1 mg (during CPR), then every 3–5 minutes
    - **After 3 shocks:** give IV amiodarone 300 mg (second dose 150 mg after 5 shocks if refractory)
    - If core temperature <30°C, limit shocks to 3 and withhold drugs until >30°C (per local hypothermia arrest guidelines)
  - Secure airway (ET tube or supraglottic) once CPR is established
  - IV access (ideally central or large-bore peripheral)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Post-ROSC care:**
    - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C for 24 hours if unresponsive)
    - Identify and treat underlying cause: **4Hs / 4Ts** (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis)
    - Coronary angiography (if STEMI or high suspicion) ± PCI
    - Invasive blood pressure monitoring, central venous access
    - Maintain normoxia, normocapnia; avoid hyperoxia (target SpO₂ 94–98%)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - ICD implantation (secondary prevention) – discuss with cardiology
  - Antiarrhythmics: amiodarone, sotalol (if ICD not tolerated/declined)
  - Consider left ventricular ejection fraction assessment (echo) – if ≤35%, ICD indicated
  - Genetic counselling (if suspect channelopathy, e.g. Brugada, LQTS)
- **Default Orders (if admitted post-ROSC):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) if hypotensive, titrate to MAP >65 mmHg
  - VTE prophylaxis: delayed until no active bleeding, then LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) per local policy
  - Diet: nil by mouth until extubated (or if not intubated, clear fluids only until swallowing assessed)
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring + hourly neuro observations (GCS, pupils) for first 24 hours; then 4-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (during arrest):**
  - **Adrenaline 1 mg IV (1:10,000 pre-filled syringe)** – give after 3rd shock, then every 3–5 minutes; [check local policy for timing]
  - **Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (given over 2–3 minutes, diluted in 20 mL 5% dextrose) – after 3rd shock; repeat once: **150 mg IV** after 5th shock
- **Alternative (if amiodarone unavailable or contraindicated):**
  - **Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV** (first dose) – then 0.5–0.75 mg/kg after 5 min; total max 3 mg/kg – [check local policy]
- **Post-ROSC / long-term antiarrhythmics:**
  - **Amiodarone** – oral loading: 200 mg three times daily for 1 week, then 200 mg twice daily for 1 week, then 200 mg daily; IV loading if recurrent arrhythmia
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Amiodarone: known severe bradycardia/heart block (unless paced), pre-existing thyroid disease; interacts with warfarin (increase INR), digoxin (increase digoxin levels), simvastatin (risk myopathy)
  - Lidocaine: severe SA/AV block, Stokes-Adams syndrome; caution in hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Amiodarone:** no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce maintenance)
  - **Lidocaine:** reduced clearance with hepatic failure – reduce dose, monitor levels
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Adrenaline: giving it *before* the first 3 defibrillations in a shockable rhythm** – may increase myocardial injury; only give after 3 shocks per ALS algorithm.
  - **Amiodarone: giving a second 300 mg dose (total 600 mg) too rapidly** – risk of severe hypotension and bradycardia; the second dose is 150 mg, not another 300 mg.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Recurrent VF/pulseless VT during arrest
  - Post-cardiac arrest syndrome: myocardial dysfunction, hypotension, anoxic brain injury, hyperkalaemia, acidosis
  - Rib fractures / pneumothorax from CPR
- **Chronic:**
  - Impaired left ventricular function (ischaemic or non-ischaemic)
  - Anoxic brain injury with neurological deficits (cognitive, movement)
  - Need for long-term amiodarone (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic toxicity)
  - Recurrent arrhythmia if underlying substrate untreated (ICD shocks)
sections.Prognosis:
- Survival to hospital discharge for witnessed VF with rapid defibrillation: ~30–40% (best of all arrest rhythms). For unwitnessed: <10%.
- Favourable neurological outcome correlates with short arrest duration, early ROSC, and absence of severe lactic acidosis (pH >7.2, lactate <5 mmol/L) – use this to guide prognostication at 72 hours.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always visualise the rhythm on the monitor *and* feel for a pulse before each defibrillator charge** – this prevents shocking VF that has become asystole or shocking a perfusing rhythm.
- **Give amiodarone through a different IV line from adrenaline** – incompatible; flush between doses.
- **In refractor VF after 3 shocks, consider adrenaline 1 mg plus a second antiarrhythmic (e.g. lidocaine) only if amiodarone already given** – discuss with senior.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay defibrillation for IV access, airway placement, or drug administration** – every minute delay reduces survival by 10%.
- **Do not stop chest compressions for >10 seconds during rhythm check** – if pulse check is uncertain, resume CPR immediately.
- **Do not confuse fine VF with asystole** – if there is any waveform, treat as shockable; you cannot harm a patient in asystole by shocking (though it is ineffective).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  1. Palpitations, syncope, or pre-syncope
  2. Inappropriate ICD shocks (if implanted) – >2 in 24 hours or any shock with loss of consciousness
  3. Signs of heart failure (worsening breathlessness, orthopnoea, oedema)
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks of discharge; ICD clinic for device interrogation at 4–6 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Immediate cardiac arrest team:** call 2222 (UK) at time of collapse
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - ROSC achieved but GCS <8 (not obeying commands)
  - Lactate >5 mmol/L or pH <7.1 after ROSC
  - Refractory hypotension requiring >2 vasopressors
  - Recurrent VF/VT despite amiodarone
  - Signs of end-organ failure (anuria, new ST-elevation, worsening respiratory failure)
- **Crash call again** if new episode of VF after initial ROSC

rawMarkdown:
## Arrhythmia — Ventricular Fibrillation
**One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm; chaotic ventricular activity; immediate defibrillation is the only priority – survival drops ~10% per minute.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Asystole mimicking fine VF** – A flat line in one lead may be fine VF in another. **If any doubt, treat as VF and shock** (you cannot worsen asystole). Check lead II and aVF, increase gain.
- **Pseudo-VF from lead artifact / motion** – The patient will have a pulse or normal arterial line trace. **Never shock a patient with a pulse.** Feel for a carotid pulse before each rhythm check if the monitor looks like VF but patient is talking.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden collapse, unconsciousness, no response
  - Agonal respirations or absent breathing
  - Palpitations / sense of impending doom seconds before (rarely witnessed)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No palpable carotid pulse
  - Unresponsive – no eye opening, no verbal response, no motor response
  - Pupils dilated and fixed (after ~2 minutes)
  - Cyanosis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Ventricular Fibrillation (chaotic, irregular, no QRS)
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia** (regular wide QRS, usually monomorphic or polymorphic – same treatment: defibrillation)
- **Asystole** (flat line, may be confused with fine VF – use alternative lead, increase gain)
- **Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)** (organized rhythm but no pulse – treat underlying cause, do not shock)
- **Splitter:** **ECG rhythm strip** – coarse VF shows completely disorganised electrical activity; pulseless VT shows organised, regular wide-complex tachycardia. If any doubt, treat as shockable rhythm.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Immediate rhythm check via defibrillator pads – **do not delay defibrillation**
  - Check pulse manually before each defibrillator rhythm analysis
  - Confirm lead placement (RA, LA, RL, LL) if using a monitor – loose leads cause artifact
- **Bloods (post-ROSC / during arrest if IV access available):**
  - VBG/ABG (pH, lactate, pCO₂, K⁺)
  - U&Es (especially K⁺, Mg²⁺)
  - Troponin (if ischaemic cause suspected)
  - Full blood count, coagulation (after ROSC)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None during arrest – do not move patient for X-ray
  - **After ROSC:** CXR (check ET tube position, pneumothorax), CT head if neurological concern, coronary angiography if STEMI suspected
- **Special tests:** Echocardiography (done during pause in CPR if trained – look for tamponade, massive PE, LV function)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** VF is defined by ECG – no consensus scoring system; diagnosis is electrocardiographic
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG rhythm strip** – before charging, **confirm rhythm is truly VF (not artifact, not asystole)** and that the patient has no pulse. A single missed rhythm check can lead to shock in a perfusing rhythm or failure to shock a shockable rhythm.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Follow the ALS algorithm:**
    - Call for help / cardiac arrest team
    - Start CPR (30:2) immediately
    - Attach defibrillator pads (sternum-apex or anterior-posterior)
    - **Defibrillate as soon as VF/VT confirmed** – biphasic: 200 J (then 150–360 J per manufacturer)
    - After each shock, immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes before rhythm check
    - **After 3 shocks:** give IV adrenaline 1 mg (during CPR), then every 3–5 minutes
    - **After 3 shocks:** give IV amiodarone 300 mg (second dose 150 mg after 5 shocks if refractory)
    - If core temperature <30°C, limit shocks to 3 and withhold drugs until >30°C (per local hypothermia arrest guidelines)
  - Secure airway (ET tube or supraglottic) once CPR is established
  - IV access (ideally central or large-bore peripheral)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Post-ROSC care:**
    - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C for 24 hours if unresponsive)
    - Identify and treat underlying cause: **4Hs / 4Ts** (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis)
    - Coronary angiography (if STEMI or high suspicion) ± PCI
    - Invasive blood pressure monitoring, central venous access
    - Maintain normoxia, normocapnia; avoid hyperoxia (target SpO₂ 94–98%)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - ICD implantation (secondary prevention) – discuss with cardiology
  - Antiarrhythmics: amiodarone, sotalol (if ICD not tolerated/declined)
  - Consider left ventricular ejection fraction assessment (echo) – if ≤35%, ICD indicated
  - Genetic counselling (if suspect channelopathy, e.g. Brugada, LQTS)
- **Default Orders (if admitted post-ROSC):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) if hypotensive, titrate to MAP >65 mmHg
  - VTE prophylaxis: delayed until no active bleeding, then LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) per local policy
  - Diet: nil by mouth until extubated (or if not intubated, clear fluids only until swallowing assessed)
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring + hourly neuro observations (GCS, pupils) for first 24 hours; then 4-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (during arrest):**
  - **Adrenaline 1 mg IV (1:10,000 pre-filled syringe)** – give after 3rd shock, then every 3–5 minutes; [check local policy for timing]
  - **Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (given over 2–3 minutes, diluted in 20 mL 5% dextrose) – after 3rd shock; repeat once: **150 mg IV** after 5th shock
- **Alternative (if amiodarone unavailable or contraindicated):**
  - **Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV** (first dose) – then 0.5–0.75 mg/kg after 5 min; total max 3 mg/kg – [check local policy]
- **Post-ROSC / long-term antiarrhythmics:**
  - **Amiodarone** – oral loading: 200 mg three times daily for 1 week, then 200 mg twice daily for 1 week, then 200 mg daily; IV loading if recurrent arrhythmia
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Amiodarone: known severe bradycardia/heart block (unless paced), pre-existing thyroid disease; interacts with warfarin (increase INR), digoxin (increase digoxin levels), simvastatin (risk myopathy)
  - Lidocaine: severe SA/AV block, Stokes-Adams syndrome; caution in hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Amiodarone:** no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce maintenance)
  - **Lidocaine:** reduced clearance with hepatic failure – reduce dose, monitor levels
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Adrenaline: giving it *before* the first 3 defibrillations in a shockable rhythm** – may increase myocardial injury; only give after 3 shocks per ALS algorithm.
  - **Amiodarone: giving a second 300 mg dose (total 600 mg) too rapidly** – risk of severe hypotension and bradycardia; the second dose is 150 mg, not another 300 mg.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Recurrent VF/pulseless VT during arrest
  - Post-cardiac arrest syndrome: myocardial dysfunction, hypotension, anoxic brain injury, hyperkalaemia, acidosis
  - Rib fractures / pneumothorax from CPR
- **Chronic:**
  - Impaired left ventricular function (ischaemic or non-ischaemic)
  - Anoxic brain injury with neurological deficits (cognitive, movement)
  - Need for long-term amiodarone (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic toxicity)
  - Recurrent arrhythmia if underlying substrate untreated (ICD shocks)

### Prognosis
- Survival to hospital discharge for witnessed VF with rapid defibrillation: ~30–40% (best of all arrest rhythms). For unwitnessed: <10%.
- Favourable neurological outcome correlates with short arrest duration, early ROSC, and absence of severe lactic acidosis (pH >7.2, lactate <5 mmol/L) – use this to guide prognostication at 72 hours.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always visualise the rhythm on the monitor *and* feel for a pulse before each defibrillator charge** – this prevents shocking VF that has become asystole or shocking a perfusing rhythm.
- **Give amiodarone through a different IV line from adrenaline** – incompatible; flush between doses.
- **In refractor VF after 3 shocks, consider adrenaline 1 mg plus a second antiarrhythmic (e.g. lidocaine) only if amiodarone already given** – discuss with senior.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay defibrillation for IV access, airway placement, or drug administration** – every minute delay reduces survival by 10%.
- **Do not stop chest compressions for >10 seconds during rhythm check** – if pulse check is uncertain, resume CPR immediately.
- **Do not confuse fine VF with asystole** – if there is any waveform, treat as shockable; you cannot harm a patient in asystole by shocking (though it is ineffective).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  1. Palpitations, syncope, or pre-syncope
  2. Inappropriate ICD shocks (if implanted) – >2 in 24 hours or any shock with loss of consciousness
  3. Signs of heart failure (worsening breathlessness, orthopnoea, oedema)
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks of discharge; ICD clinic for device interrogation at 4–6 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Immediate cardiac arrest team:** call 2222 (UK) at time of collapse
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - ROSC achieved but GCS <8 (not obeying commands)
  - Lactate >5 mmol/L or pH <7.1 after ROSC
  - Refractory hypotension requiring >2 vasopressors
  - Recurrent VF/VT despite amiodarone
  - Signs of end-organ failure (anuria, new ST-elevation, worsening respiratory failure)
- **Crash call again** if new episode of VF after initial ROSC
